description: Vocalizations were not detected, and not mentioned by any later observers of the bird either. Visual observations from binocular views and photograph inspections afterwards include; solid red head and breast except for small white spot between bill and eye. No black border on the breast, or white markings extending below or above the eyes. The rest of the underparts are fairly non-distinct for sapsuckers; yellowish belly peaking out from a mess of mottled white and grayish feathers covering the sides creating a sort of "zigzag" pattern, and whitish undertail marked with some sparse blackish streaking. The black back displayed two narrow columns of neat pale markings of a dull golden tinge. Wing pattern consisted of tertials tipped in white crescents and primaries relatively unmarked compared to closely related sapsucker taxa, but showing white bars that seemingly became more prevalent towards the outer feathers (towards tenth primary). When feathers from the bird's flanks weren't overlapping with its folded wings, the broad white bar lining the secondary coverts (representing the shoulder area to about halfway down the wing when folded) was visible. Tail feathers were jet black excepting the two large wedge-shaped very inner rectrices, which each had two small white spots on the inner side of the shaft.
photographer: Photographer: David Tonnessen, David Tonnessen, David Tonnessen, David Tonnessen
